Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic because I work part-time and have limited resources at home. I tried to carve out time on weekends and late evenings when I could actually focus. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically write things out and practice problems hands-on, so I'd often set up at the library where I could spread out my materials. I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but it was rarely consistent blocks of time—more like grabbing an hour here and there whenever I could.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. When deadlines approached, I'd cram in extra study sessions, sometimes staying up way too late. During quieter weeks, I tried to be more regular, but life always seemed to throw something at me. The stress of juggling everything meant I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule, but I did my best to stay on top of things.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was something due soon or when I wanted to check if any new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd get notifications on my phone that reminded me to check in. I also logged in frequently to participate in discussions because connecting with other students helped me feel less isolated. The anxiety of missing something important kept me checking pretty regularly, honestly.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic—like checking my phone in the morning. I made it a habit to log in during my lunch break at work and again before bed. The discussion forums became something I actually looked forward to because I'd built some connections with classmates there. It felt less like a chore and more like part of my daily rhythm once I got into it.
Q5: The discussion forums were incredibly valuable for me because I learn better when I can talk things through with others. I also found the video content helpful, though I often had to watch sections multiple times to really grasp concepts. Anything that let me engage actively rather than just passively reading was more effective for my learning style. The peer interactions especially helped me understand different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I tended to skip the longer reading materials, especially PDFs that were really text-heavy. With my limited time and high stress levels, I had to prioritize what would give me the most understanding in the least time. Some of the supplementary resources felt nice-to-have rather than essential, so I'd tell myself I'd get to them later—but later rarely came. I just couldn't afford to spend time on things that weren't directly tied to assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to improving my understanding or my grade. If I could physically practice something or discuss it with peers, that made it feel more valuable. I also appreciated activities that gave me feedback so I knew whether I was on the right track. Time is precious when you're stretched thin, so anything that felt like busywork got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they carried this weight of guilt because I knew they could help, but I often couldn't justify the time. Required activities had clear stakes, so I prioritized them even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe next week when things calm down,' but things never really calmed down. The anxiety of not completing them added to my stress, even though they weren't mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on paper because writing things out by hand helps me remember better. I used my own notebooks extensively, creating summaries and practice problems. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources online that explained concepts differently than our course materials. None of that showed up in the LMS at all.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook or practicing concepts physically, but the LMS would only show that I'd logged in briefly to download a file. I'd study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and working through difficult material together. My best learning often happened away from the screen, using printed materials or handwritten notes. The system couldn't capture those late-night study sessions at my kitchen table.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then work with that material offline for days. Internet access isn't always reliable for me, and I preferred studying with physical materials anyway. I'd watch a lecture video once online, but then review my handwritten notes from it multiple times. The LMS only saw that single access, not the hours of offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when work got busier and my anxiety spiked. I remember a few weeks where I felt completely overwhelmed—assignments piling up, not enough sleep, constant worry about money. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt exhausting because I knew I'd see all the things I was behind on. The mindfulness exercises I learned helped me push through, but it was genuinely hard.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't actually processed any of it because my mind was elsewhere. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled my attention—I'd be reading course material while mentally calculating whether I could afford groceries that week. The fatigue was real and it absolutely affected my engagement quality.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. There were sessions where I'd have the materials open but I was just going through the motions, too tired or anxious to really engage. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, or have a video playing while my mind wandered to other worries. Those moments felt frustrating because I wanted to learn but my brain just wasn't cooperating. The LMS would show activity, but it wasn't meaningful learning.
Q15: I postponed several assignments even knowing they were important, usually because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. The anxiety would build up and make it harder to begin, which created this vicious cycle. Sometimes I avoided difficult topics because I was afraid of confirming that I didn't understand them. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace to tackle them properly, but that perfect moment rarely arrived.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator and organizer. I'd work backward from due dates to figure out when I absolutely had to start something. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before assignments were due. It wasn't ideal, but with everything else going on in my life, deadlines gave me the structure I needed to prioritize. Without them, I might have spread myself too thin trying to do everything at once.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me—when I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more and reassured me I was on the right track. Constructive criticism helped me focus my limited study time on areas that needed improvement. Sometimes waiting for feedback created anxiety, and I'd check the LMS repeatedly hoping to see my grades. Good feedback after struggling with something was incredibly validating and pushed me to keep going.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap to follow. But sometimes the pacing felt relentless, like there wasn't enough breathing room between major assignments. I appreciated when the structure allowed flexibility in how we demonstrated our learning. The discussion requirements actually helped me stay connected and accountable, which I needed given my tendency to isolate when stressed.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because they gave me a reason to connect with others and think through ideas collaboratively. However, the sheer volume of materials sometimes felt overwhelming, making it hard to know what to prioritize. Having videos available was great, but clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped. The assignment structure was manageable, though tighter feedback turnaround would have reduced my anxiety.
Q20: I think that pattern makes total sense actually. Someone might be logging in frequently, watching videos, reading materials—genuinely trying to learn—but struggling to complete assignments due to time constraints, confusion, or perfectionism. High activity could also mean someone is searching for help or trying to understand requirements but hitting barriers. Life circumstances might allow for browsing but not for the focused time needed to complete substantial work.
Q21: This resonates with me somewhat. I know my attendance was low but I still passed because I made the most of my study time outside the LMS. Someone might be efficiently learning offline, using downloaded materials or external resources, and only logging in when absolutely necessary. It could also reflect different learning styles—some people don't need to engage with every online resource to understand the material. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns of when students are active and which resources they access. They're good at tracking completion of specific tasks and participation in discussions. But they completely miss the depth of engagement—whether someone is truly learning or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the quality of understanding. A student could look disengaged in the data but be working incredibly hard in ways the system doesn't see.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's about actively thinking, questioning, and connecting ideas rather than passively consuming content. For me, engagement includes discussing concepts with peers, applying knowledge to real situations, and persisting even when things get difficult. It's a mental and emotional investment in learning, which doesn't always translate to clicks and login times.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I struggled with anxiety while trying to learn, or how the mindfulness techniques helped me push through difficult moments. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes, the study sessions with classmates over coffee, or the times I taught concepts to myself out loud while doing dishes. My resilience—getting back up after setbacks, continuing despite limited resources and high stress—that's invisible in the data but it's central to my experience.
